articles.title: Joint-specific measures improve risk adjustment in total knee arthroplasty: A machine learning approach.
articles.authors: Muller D, Gillani A, Hirschmann MT, Lazic I, Hinterwimmer F, Arber A, et al.
articles.study_type: Retrospective cohort study using machine learning
articles.population: Patients undergoing primary total knee arthroplasty
articles.sample_size: 1207 procedures at a single academic centre between 2018 and 2022
articles.outcomes: Postoperative complications and residual pain at 1 year following total knee arthroplasty
articles.take_home: Adding information about the knee joint itself, such as bone defects and how much it can bend, improved doctors' ability to predict which patients would have complications after knee replacement surgery. However, this extra information did not help predict which patients would still have pain after surgery.

articles.title: Slope reducing osteotomy: The emerging gap between indication, safety and proportionality.
articles.authors: Pineda T, Seil R, Sonnery-Cottet B, Dejour DH, Hirschmann MT, Ollivier M
articles.population: Patients with anterior cruciate ligament injuries, particularly high-risk patients and those needing revision surgery
articles.outcomes: Appropriate use and safety of slope-reducing osteotomy based on risk stratification and patient selection
articles.take_home: Slope-reducing osteotomy is becoming popular for ACL injuries but is being used before there is enough evidence to guide when and how to use it. Doctors should base decisions on individual patient risk factors rather than simple numerical thresholds, and surgical experience remains important for safe use.

articles.title: No clinical and functional benefit after medial congruent compared to ultra congruent total knee arthroplasty at 1 year: A prospective randomized controlled study.
articles.authors: Schrednitzki D, Sina J, Lamarche A, Meissner N, Halder AM
articles.population: Patients with advanced varus knee osteoarthritis undergoing primary total knee arthroplasty
articles.sample_size: 80 patients randomised; 76 completed 12-month follow-up (38 in each group)
articles.outcomes: Primary: Knee Society Score. Secondary: range of motion, Oxford Knee Score, Forgotten Joint Score, High-Activity Arthroplasty Score, patient satisfaction and willingness to repeat procedure
articles.take_home: Both types of knee replacement insert (medial congruent and ultra congruent) produced excellent results at one year with high patient satisfaction, and there were no meaningful differences between them. Either design can be used based on surgeon preference.

articles.title: Knee arthroplasty in patients under 50 years demonstrates durable survivorship despite substantially higher lifetime revision risk than in older patients.
articles.authors: Morrison SR, Hall AJ, Lim B, Akhtar A, Walmsley P
articles.study_type: Retrospective analysis of prospectively collected registry data
articles.population: Patients aged 16-49 years undergoing primary knee replacement, compared with patients aged 50 years and older
articles.sample_size: 3069 patients aged under 50 years (58.6% female, mean age 44); date-matched comparison cohort aged 50 and over
articles.outcomes: Revision rate, time to revision, mortality, cumulative incidence of revision, estimated lifetime revision risk
articles.take_home: Knee replacements in patients under 50 years of age work well in the long term, but around 30% may eventually need revision surgery compared with only 15% in older patients. Patients under 50 should be counselled about this higher lifetime risk of needing further surgery.

articles.title: Moderate to high levels of spin in studies comparing open and arthroscopic Latarjet procedures for anterior shoulder instability: A systematic review from the LaTour Group.
articles.authors: Akrivos VS, Claizergues B, Collin P, Levy G, Valencia GV, van Rooij F, et al.
articles.population: Studies comparing open and arthroscopic Latarjet procedures for anterior shoulder instability
articles.outcomes: Prevalence, type and level of spin (misleading reporting, misleading interpretation, inadequate extrapolation) in comparative studies
articles.take_home: 80% of studies comparing open and arthroscopic Latarjet procedures contained at least one type of misleading reporting or interpretation, with selective reporting of results being the most common problem. Readers should be cautious when interpreting these studies and look for transparent reporting and discussion of limitations.

articles.title: Highly variable proximal tibia anatomy causes controversies for proper tibial component rotational alignment in total knee arthroplasty: A current concept review.
articles.authors: Hofmann S, Tecame A, Hirschmann M, Lustig S, Seitlinger G, Innocenti B
articles.population: Patients undergoing total knee arthroplasty for osteoarthritis
articles.outcomes: Tibial component rotational alignment methods, patellar tracking, functional outcome, anterior knee pain
articles.take_home: Surgeons have many different approaches to positioning the tibial component during knee replacement, but no single method has been proven better than others. The key is identifying the 30% of patients with abnormal tibia positioning and correcting it during surgery.

articles.title: Comparative Analysis of Clinically Meaningful Recovery Trajectories Following Unicompartmental Arthroplasty and Patello-Femoral Arthroplasty.
articles.authors: Singh R, Williams DL, Alexander J, Marrache M, Bedair HS, Melnic CM
articles.population: Patients with isolated compartmental knee osteoarthritis treated with medial unicompartmental arthroplasty, lateral unicompartmental arthroplasty, or patello-femoral arthroplasty
articles.sample_size: 2,487 patients (1,938 medial UKA, 283 lateral UKA, 256 PFA)
articles.outcomes: Time to minimal clinically important difference in patient-reported outcome measures (KOOS-PS, PROMIS Global Physical Health, PROMIS PF-10a); conversion to total knee arthroplasty; non-conversion reoperation rates
articles.take_home: All three types of partial knee replacement surgery showed similar speed of functional recovery in the early period, with patients reaching meaningful improvement within 1 to 2 months. Patello-femoral arthroplasty had higher rates of non-conversion reoperations at two years, which may be important when choosing between procedures.

articles.title: Propensity Score-Matched Comparison of Patient-Reported Outcomes Between Crowe I Developmental Dysplasia of the Hip and Primary Osteoarthritis Following Total Hip Arthroplasty.
articles.authors: Saluja A, Eschert J, Ren R, Wong Z, Su E
articles.population: Patients undergoing total hip arthroplasty for either Crowe I developmental dysplasia of the hip or primary osteoarthritis
articles.sample_size: 210 hips (105 with developmental dysplasia, 105 with osteoarthritis)
articles.outcomes: Modified Harris Hip Score, Hip disability and Osteoarthritis Outcome Score - Joint Replacement, pain on Visual Analog Scale, activity level on UCLA Activity Scale, revision rates, and implant survivorship
articles.take_home: Patients with Crowe I developmental dysplasia of the hip achieved similar pain relief, function, and implant survival to those with osteoarthritis after hip replacement surgery. The quality of life before surgery, rather than the underlying hip condition, was the main factor determining how much patients benefited.

articles.title: What Factors Increase the Risk of Dislocation after Primary Total Hip Arthroplasty Using the Direct Anterior Approach?
articles.authors: Murphy MP, Forti CR, Ho H, Hopper RH Jr, Hamilton WG
articles.study_type: Retrospective cohort analysis with matched case-control comparison
articles.population: Patients undergoing primary total hip arthroplasty via direct anterior approach
articles.sample_size: 8,658 primary THAs (45 dislocations, with matched controls for radiographic analysis)
articles.outcomes: Dislocation rate and associated risk factors including patient demographics, implant characteristics, and cup positioning parameters
articles.take_home: Women having hip replacement via the direct anterior approach face higher dislocation risk, particularly those receiving smaller implant heads (28 mm or 32 mm). Surgeons should pay particular attention to precise cup positioning during surgery to minimise this risk.

articles.title: Robotic Total Hip Arthroplasty in Atypical Hip Anatomy: Accuracy of Component Positioning and Clinical Outcomes in 192 Complex Cases.
articles.authors: Kayani B, Enson J, Donaldson J, Miles J, Newman S, Jayadev C, et al.
articles.population: Patients with atypical hip anatomy undergoing robotic total hip replacement, including developmental dysplasia of the hip, Leg-Calve-Perthes disease, slipped capital femoral epiphysis, previous acetabular fracture, and skeletal dysplasia
articles.outcomes: Component positioning accuracy, patient satisfaction, hip function scores (UCLA, WOMAC, Oxford Hip Score, Forgotten Joint Score), component survivorship at 5 years, and complications
articles.take_home: Robotic hip replacement was very accurate at positioning components in patients with complex hip anatomy, achieving target positions within 1-3 mm and resulting in 98.8% of implants surviving 5 years without revision. Patients reported high satisfaction and significant improvements in hip function and activity levels.

articles.title: Social Determinants of Health Z-Codes are Associated with Low Compliance with Patient-Reported Outcome Measures after Total Hip Arthroplasty.
articles.authors: Parry M, Ozdag Y, Kloc A, Salvato J, Mercuri JJ
articles.population: Patients undergoing elective total hip arthroplasty between 2017 and 2022
articles.outcomes: Compliance rates with patient-reported outcome measures (PROMs) at two years after surgery, assessed against social determinants of health codes, mental health codes, and demographic factors
articles.take_home: Only 25.8% of patients completed patient-reported outcome questionnaires after hip replacement surgery. Patients with social risk factors, non-English speakers, and men were less likely to complete these questionnaires, whilst employed and married patients were more likely to fill them out.

articles.title: Differences Between Inpatient and Outpatient Medicare Total Knee Arthroplasty: Substantial Clinical Benefit Thresholds on Knee Injury and Osteoarthritis Outcome Score for Joint Replacement.
articles.authors: Patel SV, Pasqualini I, Khan ST, Elmenawi KA, Zhang C, Piuzzi NS
articles.study_type: Retrospective analysis of prospectively collected data
articles.population: Medicare beneficiaries aged 65 years and over who had primary elective total knee replacement
articles.outcomes: Knee injury and Osteoarthritis Outcome Score for Joint Replacement (KOOS-JR) substantial clinical benefit thresholds at 29.3 points for inpatient and 21.5 points for outpatient settings
articles.take_home: The study found that inpatients need to improve by nearly 8 more points on the KOOS-JR scale than outpatients to show meaningful benefit after knee replacement, which may reflect differences in how ill patients are when they start treatment. Using different improvement targets for inpatient versus outpatient surgery could provide fairer performance assessment.

articles.title: Risk and Benefit Profile with Concurrent Nonsteroidal Anti-Inflammatory Drugs and Direct Oral Anticoagulants Following Total Knee Arthroplasty.
articles.authors: Paul BR, Verhey JT, Haak GM, Braithwaite CL, Tarabichi S, Christopher ZK, et al.
articles.study_type: Retrospective cohort study using national claims database
articles.population: Patients undergoing primary total knee arthroplasty who received direct oral anticoagulants for blood clot prevention
articles.sample_size: 49,860 patients (12,733 receiving NSAIDs plus anticoagulant, 37,127 receiving anticoagulant alone)
articles.outcomes: 90-day wound complications, surgical site infection, periprosthetic joint infection, bleeding events, two-year revision rates, arthrofibrosis and manipulation under anaesthesia
articles.take_home: Taking NSAIDs together with anticoagulant drugs after knee replacement was linked to higher rates of wound problems, infections and need for revision surgery, but did reduce stiffness. The choice of which NSAID matters, with some types carrying higher infection risks than others.

articles.title: Complications of Ceramic-on-Ceramic Total Hip Arthroplasty with Large Heads: 10-Year Minimum Follow-Up.
articles.authors: Lee DH, Park JW, Jung WH, Kim TY, Ha YC, Koo KH, et al.
articles.population: Patients undergoing cementless total hip replacement with ceramic-on-ceramic bearings
articles.sample_size: 178 patients (201 hips) followed for minimum 10 years
articles.outcomes: Ceramic fracture, dislocation, clinical hip scores, bone loss (osteolysis), implant noise, revision surgery rates
articles.take_home: Large ceramic hip replacements did not fracture and had very low revision rates (99.1% survival) over 10 years, but late dislocation and implant noise remained occasional concerns, particularly in patients with spine problems.

articles.title: Disparities in Preoperative Weight Loss and Obesity Treatment Before Total Joint Arthroplasty.
articles.authors: Seward MW, Grimm JA, Bedard NA, Hannon CP, Berry DJ, Abdel MP
articles.population: Patients undergoing total hip or knee replacement with BMI of 30 or above
articles.outcomes: Preoperative obesity treatment use, weight loss achievement, and disparities by socioeconomic status and sex
articles.take_home: Very few patients received obesity treatment before joint replacement surgery, with only 0.2% receiving weight loss medications and 1.6% receiving nutrition services. Women and patients from more deprived areas were more likely to have higher BMI before surgery but were less likely to lose weight, highlighting unfair effects of BMI-based surgery cutoffs.

articles.title: Risk factors for asseptic loosening after revision total knee replacement (rTKR): a component-specific analysis of femoral and tibial reconstruction.
articles.authors: Souto CS, Pereira F, Carvalho A, Sousa R
articles.population: Patients undergoing revision total knee replacement for aseptic loosening
articles.sample_size: 215 revision total knee replacements with 2-year minimum follow-up
articles.outcomes: Re-revision rate (primary); radiographic fixation assessed using D'Amato scoring system; association between reconstruction methods (augments and metaphyseal sleeves) and re-revision
articles.take_home: Tibial augments were associated with higher rates of re-revision (5.6% overall), whilst metaphyseal sleeves showed lower failure rates. The study suggests that achieving adequate reconstruction and stable fixation is important in revision knee replacement.

articles.title: Construction of a prediction model for isolated distal deep vein thrombosis after total knee arthroplasty based on machine learning.
articles.authors: Chen JH, Zhang ZF, Guo SH
articles.study_type: Prediction model development study using machine learning
articles.population: Patients undergoing total knee arthroplasty
articles.outcomes: Incidence of isolated distal deep vein thrombosis within three days after total knee arthroplasty; prediction model performance measured by AUC
articles.take_home: About 15 in 100 patients developed blood clots in the lower leg veins within three days of knee replacement surgery. Researchers created a computer model using seven patient characteristics that can predict which patients are at higher risk of developing these clots, which could help doctors identify and treat at-risk patients earlier.

articles.title: Evaluation of the performance of large language models in the management of hip and knee osteoarthritis: analysis of EULAR 2023 recommendations.
articles.authors: Mirza Y, Sari F, Arslan S
articles.study_type: Comparative analysis of large language models against clinical guidelines
articles.population: Not applicable; study evaluated artificial intelligence systems rather than patients
articles.sample_size: Three large language models tested (DeepSeek-R1, Gemini 2.5 Flash, ChatGPT-5) against eight EULAR recommendations
articles.outcomes: Informational quality, reliability, usability, readability, semantic similarity and lexical similarity to EULAR 2023 recommendations for hip and knee osteoarthritis management
articles.take_home: DeepSeek-R1 performed better than other AI systems at answering questions about osteoarthritis management based on expert guidelines, but all three systems need improvement in readability and should only be used as tools supervised by healthcare professionals, not by patients independently.

articles.title: A System in Motion: The Evolution of Orthopaedic Care in Romania.
articles.authors: Dragosloveanu S, Nedelea DG, Scheau C
articles.population: Romanian orthopaedic patients
articles.outcomes: Digital infrastructure adoption, robotic-assisted surgery implementation, artificial intelligence applications, hospital bed capacity, arthroplasty registry modernisation
articles.take_home: Romania has increased its use of digital tools and robotic surgery in orthopaedic care, and has high hospital bed capacity to support treatment of elderly and complex patients. The country has modernised its arthroplasty register to match standards in other European countries.

articles.title: Salvage total hip arthroplasty after internal fixation compared with acute total hip arthroplasty for fracture: a cohort study based on 32,960 cases from the Dutch Arthroplasty Register.
articles.authors: Van Marle L, Peters RM, Van Steenbergen LN, Jaarsma RL, Doornberg JN, Zijlstra WP
articles.population: Patients who had total hip replacement for hip fracture, either as an immediate procedure or after failed internal fixation
articles.sample_size: 32,960 procedures (12,486 salvage procedures after failed fixation; 18,450 acute procedures)
articles.outcomes: Rates of revision hip replacement surgery needed within 1 and 5 years; reasons for revision including infection, loosening and dislocation
articles.take_home: Hip replacements done after failed internal fixation had similar revision rates to hip replacements done immediately after fracture, suggesting that previous fixation attempts do not make the outcome worse. Both groups had about 4 to 5 per cent needing revision surgery within 5 years.

articles.title: A multivariable MRI-based risk model for medial meniscal extrusion progression and treatment failure.
articles.authors: Endara Urresta F, Penaherrera-Carrillo C, Barros Castro A, Zuniga S
articles.study_type: Observational cohort study with internal validation
articles.population: Patients with medial knee pain and medial meniscal displacement on MRI
articles.outcomes: Treatment failure (major subsequent surgery, meniscal reintervention, or meaningful deterioration in patient-reported outcomes) at mean follow-up of 32 months
articles.take_home: Researchers developed a new MRI-based model that combines measurements of meniscus displacement with other knee factors to predict which patients will need further surgery or experience worsening symptoms. The model identified low-risk (12% failure), intermediate-risk (34% failure), and high-risk (64% failure) groups, helping doctors make better treatment decisions.

articles.title: Comparison of isokinetic strength recovery following ACL reconstruction among graft types.
articles.authors: Schlangen E, Wenzlaff S, Swetkovich R, Arenz A, Miller B, Murphy R, et al.
articles.population: Patients aged 16-35 undergoing primary ACL reconstruction
articles.sample_size: 262 participants (134 hamstring tendon, 67 quadriceps tendon, 27 bone-patellar tendon-bone, 34 allograft)
articles.outcomes: Isokinetic quadriceps and hamstring muscle strength measured at 3, 6, 9, and 12 months after surgery
articles.take_home: The type of graft used in ACL reconstruction affects how quickly different muscles recover their strength. Hamstring and allograft grafts helped the thigh muscle recover faster, but hamstring grafts led to weaker hamstring muscles afterwards due to where the graft was taken from.

articles.title: A novel stepwise lateral patellar facetectomy technique in total knee arthroplasty: a comparative clinical and radiological study.
articles.authors: Ozer M, Ardali D, Birinci M, Kemah B, Polat O, Camur S
articles.population: Patients undergoing total knee arthroplasty
articles.sample_size: 203 patients (99 lateral patellar facetectomy group, 104 non-facetectomy group)
articles.outcomes: Knee Society Score, Oxford score, Kujala score, patellar displacement, lateral displacement, congruence angle, tilt angle
articles.take_home: Patients who had a new technique called lateral patellar facetectomy during knee replacement surgery had better alignment of the kneecap on X-rays and scored higher on pain and function tests compared to patients who had standard techniques. This suggests the new technique may help improve satisfaction after knee replacement surgery.

articles.title: Replication of the Robotic Evaluation of Articular Laxity (REAL) classification using intraoperative gap data in robot-assisted total knee arthroplasty.
articles.authors: Morita Y, Mylonakis N, Shin J, El-Hassan M, Shichman I, Kenanidis E, et al.
articles.population: Patients undergoing robot-assisted total knee arthroplasty for osteoarthritis
articles.outcomes: Distribution of mediolateral imbalance using REAL classification; medial and lateral extension and flexion gaps; patient-reported outcomes
articles.take_home: The REAL classification system for measuring knee balance during robotic knee replacement surgery was successfully replicated in this independent patient group, and intraoperative balancing procedures significantly improved the alignment of the knee joint. Patient-reported outcomes were similar regardless of initial balance category.

articles.title: 1-year patient-reported outcomes in patients with a documented GLP-1 agonist prescription history prior to total hip arthroplasty: a retrospective cohort study.
articles.authors: Benyamini B, Khan ST, Elmenawi KA, Patel SV, Deren ME, Piuzzi NS
articles.population: Patients undergoing primary total hip arthroplasty, with and without GLP-1 agonist prescription history
articles.outcomes: 1-year patient-reported outcome measures (HOOS Pain, Physical Function, Joint Replacement subscales), patient satisfaction, complication rates, hospital readmission, length of stay
articles.take_home: GLP-1 agonist use before hip replacement did not affect patient satisfaction or symptom improvement at 1 year. Patients using GLP-1 agonists had fewer 90-day hospital readmissions but more medical complications within 90 days, though the small number of patients taking GLP-1 agonists limits the strength of these findings.

articles.title: Comparative Analysis of Immune Cell-Type Abundances in Periprosthetic Tissues Across Arthroplasty Failure Etiologies: Use of Transcriptomic Deconvolution.
articles.authors: Li Y, Wang F, Li Y, Mu W, Ji B, Zhang X, et al.
articles.study_type: Comparative laboratory study using transcriptomic analysis
articles.population: Patients with failed joint replacements: 135 with periprosthetic joint infection and 50 with aseptic failure
articles.outcomes: Immune cell profiles in infected versus non-infected failed joint replacements; association between CD8+ to regulatory T cell ratio and infection recurrence
articles.take_home: Infected joint replacements show different patterns of immune cells compared to non-infected failures, with higher levels of certain T cells and inflammatory macrophages in infections. Patients with infected joints who had higher levels of CD8+ cells relative to regulatory T cells had better outcomes with lower rates of infection returning.

articles.title: A Proposed Alternative Sampling Strategy for Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services Arthroplasty Patient-Reported Outcome Performance Measure Compliance.
articles.authors: Lyman S, Chin A, Alschuler DM, Brill CO, Fontana M, MacLean C, et al.
articles.population: Hospitals performing total hip and knee replacements reporting to Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services
articles.sample_size: Modelled hospitals with 5, 50, 500 and 5,000 eligible cases annually
articles.outcomes: Minimum sample size of patient-reported outcome measures needed to produce statistically valid performance estimates with 95% confidence interval
articles.take_home: A random sampling strategy could reduce the number of patient questionnaires hospitals need to collect compared to current rules, particularly for larger hospitals, whilst still producing reliable results. This approach could reduce burden on hospitals without compromising the quality of performance measurement.

articles.title: First Revision Total Hip Arthroplasty for Instability: 10-Year Outcomes and Risk Factors Associated with Re-Revision.
articles.authors: Yapp LZ, Howard LC, Greidanus NV, Garbuz DS, Masri BA, Neufeld ME
articles.population: Patients undergoing first revision total hip replacement for instability
articles.outcomes: 10-year survival free from instability-related re-revision (82.2%) and all-cause re-revision (73.6%); risk factors for re-revision identified including implant type, patient age, BMI and abductor muscle deficiency
articles.take_home: About one in five patients having revision hip replacement for instability will experience recurrent instability within 10 years, and about one in three will need further surgery for any reason. Using dual mobility components or large femoral heads appears to reduce instability, whereas constrained liners carry higher revision risk.

articles.title: Jumbo Femoral Heads in Total Hip Arthroplasty: Improved Early Stability Without Added Complications.
articles.authors: Kim BI, Cheng R, Grabov E, Debbi EM, Vigdorchik JM, Mayman DJ, et al.
articles.population: Patients undergoing primary total hip arthroplasty
articles.sample_size: 6,413 hip replacements (5,897 with 36 mm heads; 516 with 40 or 44 mm heads)
articles.outcomes: Prosthetic hip dislocation, periprosthetic joint infection, periprosthetic fracture
articles.take_home: Jumbo femoral heads (40 or 44 mm) reduced dislocation risk slightly during the first year after surgery compared with standard large heads (36 mm), but this benefit did not last long-term. There was no increase in infection or fracture rates with jumbo heads.

articles.title: Dogma Under the Knife: Challenging the Status Quo in Primary Total Knee Arthroplasty.
articles.authors: Padgett BM, Terhune EB, Chalmers BP, Pagnano MW, Hannon CP
articles.population: Patients undergoing primary total knee arthroplasty
articles.outcomes: Patient satisfaction, pain relief, functional improvement, implant survivorship
articles.take_home: Although knee replacement is successful for most patients, about 10 to 20% remain dissatisfied even when the implant works well. Moving away from one-size-fits-all approaches towards personalised treatment decisions, including choices about alignment, fixation, bearing surfaces and kneecap treatment, may help improve patient satisfaction.

articles.title: Revision Total Joint Arthroplasty at the Ambulatory Surgery Center: A Single Institutional Experience.
articles.authors: Acuna AJ, Potluri AS, Yadav AS, Alfonsi S, Forlenza EM, Della Valle CJ
articles.population: Patients undergoing aseptic revision total joint arthroplasty at an ambulatory surgery centre
articles.sample_size: 153 patients (181 enrolled, 28 excluded for inadequate follow-up)
articles.outcomes: Complications within 90 days postoperatively, re-revision rates at final follow-up (mean 3.4 years), survival free from re-revision at 2 and 5 years
articles.take_home: Revision joint replacement surgery performed in an outpatient setting had low complication rates (11% within 90 days) and low re-revision rates (94-97% of patients did not need further surgery at 2-5 years). This suggests that with careful selection of suitable patients and procedures, revision joint replacement can be safely done outside hospital.

articles.title: The "Distal Femoral Replacement Downgrade": Technique of Using Bicondylar Femoral Cones with a Hinge Total Knee Arthroplasty Revision in Patients Who Have Massive Distal Femoral Bone Loss as an Alternative.
articles.authors: Chalmers BP, Afzal S, Bhatti P, Anatone AJ, Blevins JL, Debbi EM, et al.
articles.population: Patients undergoing revision total knee replacement for severe distal femoral bone loss
articles.outcomes: Survivorship free from revision for aseptic loosening 100% at one and two years; survivorship free from any re-revision 85.1% at one and two years; three knees required reoperation (periprosthetic fracture, infection, patellar button exchange)
articles.take_home: A bone-preserving surgical technique using femoral cones and a hinge design achieved good results in patients with severe femoral bone loss during revision knee replacement, avoiding the need for more extensive bone replacement surgery in most cases. Three out of twenty patients needed further surgery during the follow-up period.

articles.title: Long-term outcomes of a cemented cruciate-retaining total knee arthroplasty: a 10-year follow-up study.
articles.authors: Kalaai S, Schotanus MGM, van Dun B, Most J, Tilman PBJ, Van Haaren EH, et al.
articles.population: Patients undergoing cemented cruciate-retaining total knee arthroplasty
articles.outcomes: Implant survival rate, patient-reported outcome measures, radiographic findings, revision surgery rates
articles.take_home: The cemented knee replacement system studied showed excellent long-term results, with 95.4% of implants surviving without needing revision surgery at nearly 14 years follow-up and patients continuing to report improved function over time.

articles.title: Curved decelerations result in greater frontal-plane knee loading than straight-line tasks.
articles.authors: Galmiche L, Kersting UG, Bill K
articles.population: Healthy recreationally active males
articles.outcomes: Peak knee abduction moment, ground reaction force, frontal-plane moment arm during different deceleration tasks
articles.take_home: Decelerating whilst moving in a curved path places greater stress on the knee joint than straight-line deceleration, which may help explain why ACL injuries happen during sport-specific movements. This suggests that curved deceleration drills should be included in injury prevention programmes for athletes.

articles.title: Machine learning-based clinical tool for identifying factors associated with symptomatic knee osteoarthritis: the Nagahama study.
articles.authors: Taniguchi M, Nakazato K, Ikezoe T, Tsuboyama T, Ito H, Matsuda S, et al.
articles.study_type: Machine learning model development and validation study
articles.population: Adults aged 60 years and over with radiographically confirmed knee osteoarthritis
articles.outcomes: Accuracy of a machine learning-based clinical tool for identifying factors associated with symptomatic knee osteoarthritis, measured by area under the receiver operating characteristic curve
articles.take_home: Researchers developed a clinical scoring tool using machine learning that identifies eight key modifiable factors linked to symptomatic knee osteoarthritis, including low back pain, disease severity, mood, knee movement and strength, and muscle quality. The tool showed moderate accuracy in predicting which patients with knee osteoarthritis would have symptoms.

articles.title: Factors associated with substantial preoperative haemaglobin decline in hip fracture patients: a propensity-matched analysis of 1935 cases.
articles.authors: Graif N, Abdel Latif A, Warschawski Y, Elbaz E, Ben-Tov T, Snir N, et al.
articles.study_type: Retrospective observational study with propensity score matching
articles.population: Adults with hip fractures treated at a tertiary centre
articles.outcomes: Preoperative haemoglobin decline of 2 g/dL or more, blood transfusion rates, hospital length of stay
articles.take_home: Nearly half of hip fracture patients had significant blood loss before surgery. Patients with extracapsular fractures, those over 85 years old, and those waiting more than 24 hours for surgery were more likely to lose blood before the operation, and these patients needed more transfusions and stayed in hospital longer.

articles.title: Early migration following revision total knee arthroplasty with tibial metaphyseal cones: a 2-year prospective RSA cohort study of 25 patients.
articles.authors: Mills K, Belt M, Goosen JHM, Van Hellemondt GG, Heesterbeek PJC
articles.study_type: Prospective cohort study using radiostereometric analysis
articles.population: Patients undergoing revision total knee replacement with tibial metaphyseal cones
articles.outcomes: Tibial component migration measured by total translation and total rotation at 2 years; clinical pain, satisfaction and function scores; adverse events including re-revision
articles.take_home: Most tibial cones showed stable fixation with minimal movement at 2 years, but some patients had more migration than expected. One patient needed further revision surgery for loosening within the 2-year period.

articles.title: 6-week cryo-compression therapy and opioid use after unicompartmental knee arthroplasty with comparable pain scores: a secondary analysis of a randomized controlled trial.
articles.authors: Biemans H, De Vries AJ, Brouwer RW
articles.population: Adults scheduled for unicompartmental knee arthroplasty
articles.outcomes: Pain at rest and during loading, oxycodone consumption, knee function scores, quality of life, physical tests
articles.take_home: Six weeks of cryo-compression therapy after partial knee replacement did not reduce pain compared with standard care, although patients using cryo-compression took 46% less opioid painkillers overall. Quality of life improved slightly more with cryo-compression but not by clinically meaningful amounts.

articles.title: Validation of periprosthetic joint infection after total knee arthroplasty: a cohort study on 423 cases from the Norwegian Arthroplasty Register.
articles.authors: Mossige M, Lutro O, Fenstad AM, Leta TH, Bruun T, Furnes O, et al.
articles.population: Patients who had reoperation after total knee arthroplasty in Western Norway
articles.sample_size: 423 reoperations (170 reported as periprosthetic joint infection)
articles.outcomes: Sensitivity, specificity, negative and positive predictive values, and accuracy of reporting periprosthetic joint infection using the Musculoskeletal Infection Society definition
articles.take_home: Surgeons' reports of joint infections after knee replacement in the Norwegian register are accurate 94% of the time, meaning the register data can be reliably used for quality monitoring and research.

articles.title: External validation of machine learning models for estimation of mortality 1, 3, 6, and 12 months after hip fracture on 5,055 consecutive patients.
articles.authors: Mosfeldt M, Jorgensen HL, Lauritzen JB, Jansson KA
articles.study_type: External validation study of machine learning models
articles.population: Consecutive patients with hip fractures treated at 2 hospitals in Stockholm, Sweden
articles.outcomes: Mortality at 1, 3, 6, and 12 months after hip fracture
articles.take_home: Machine learning models, particularly eXtreme Gradient Boosting, can reliably predict mortality after hip fracture in an independent patient group. After adjusting the models for differences between populations, they performed well and are now available online to help clinicians give patients personalised mortality risk estimates.

articles.title: CT-based micromotion analysis for tracking fracture displacement and fragment movement in trochanteric hip fractures: an agreement study.
articles.authors: Aberg H, Hanni M, Sandberg O, Jonsson KB
articles.study_type: Prospective cohort study with agreement analysis
articles.population: Patients with trochanteric hip fractures treated with cephalomedullary nail
articles.outcomes: Measurement agreement and repeatability of CT-based micromotion analysis for quantifying fracture displacement, surgical reduction, and postoperative fragment movement
articles.take_home: CT-based micromotion analysis can precisely measure hip fracture position and movement in three dimensions with excellent repeatability. After surgery, fractures remained stable during healing with small amounts of expected movement such as shortening and inward shift of the bone.

articles.title: How Do Operating Room Timings Differ Between Anterior and Posterior Approaches for Total Hip Arthroplasty.
articles.authors: Ruff G, Di Pauli von Treuheim T, Sarfraz A, Metko K, Rozell JC, Schwarzkopf R, et al.
articles.population: Patients undergoing primary unilateral elective total hip arthroplasty
articles.outcomes: Operating room timings including positioning, implantation, closure, and total OR time between anterior and posterior approaches
articles.take_home: Anterior approach hip replacement takes about 10 minutes less total operating room time than the posterior approach, mainly due to faster setup and closure. However, cement use adds more time to anterior approach procedures than posterior approach ones.

articles.title: Factor XI and XIa Inhibitors for Venous Thromboembolism Prophylaxis After Total Knee Arthroplasty: A Review From Mechanism to Phase III Trials.
articles.authors: Smitterberg CW, Ando W, Chen AF, Garvin KL, Hamilton WG, Lee GC, et al.
articles.study_type: Literature review of Factor XI and XIa inhibitors
articles.population: Patients undergoing total knee arthroplasty
articles.outcomes: Venous thromboembolism prevention, bleeding risk, wound complications, infection risk
articles.take_home: Factor XI and XIa inhibitor drugs may reduce blood clots after knee replacement surgery while causing less bleeding than current treatments, with several new drugs being tested in large trials to confirm whether they work better than existing options.

articles.title: Intraosseous Sustained-Release Anesthetics Promote Early Ambulation and Functional Recovery after Total Knee Arthroplasty.
articles.authors: Huang J, Abdullah Ezzi SH, Wu S, Ali Alshabi MM, Zhang W, Adil MM, et al.
articles.population: Patients undergoing total knee replacement, mean age 75 years
articles.outcomes: Pain levels (VAS), pain relief medication use (PCEA time), walking distance, and knee movement range
articles.take_home: Injecting sustained-release anesthetic into the bone during knee replacement surgery, in addition to standard local anaesthetic infiltration, reduced pain and helped patients walk further and move their knee better in the first few days after surgery.

articles.title: Comparing Clinical and Radiological Outcomes for a Three-Dimensional Printed Highly-Porous Cup and a Hydroxyapatite-Coated Non-Highly-Porous Cup in Total Hip Arthroplasty Using Propensity Score Matching.
articles.authors: Okazaki T, Imagama T, Matsuki Y, Kaneoka T, Kawakami T, Sakai T
articles.study_type: Retrospective comparative study with propensity score matching
articles.population: Patients undergoing total hip arthroplasty with CT-based navigation
articles.sample_size: 132 hips (66 in each group after matching)
articles.outcomes: Clinical outcomes using JOA and JHEQ scores at 6, 12 and 24 months; radiological outcomes including cup orientation, migration, loosening and radiolucent lines
articles.take_home: Three-dimensional printed highly-porous cups and hydroxyapatite-coated cups performed similarly well after two years, with comparable pain relief and function. The highly-porous cups had more initial gaps around them but these improved over time, and both types showed no signs of loosening.

articles.title: Aspirin is Associated with Improved Venous Thromboembolism Prophylaxis Compared with Anticoagulants After Total Hip Arthroplasty in Patients Who Have Prior Hematologic Malignancy.
articles.authors: Paul BR, Verhey JT, Tarabichi S, Van Schuyver PR, Christopher ZK, Bingham JS, et al.
articles.study_type: Retrospective cohort study using national administrative database
articles.population: Patients with prior hematologic malignancy undergoing total hip arthroplasty
articles.sample_size: 1,043 patients receiving aspirin matched to 1,484 receiving non-aspirin anticoagulation
articles.outcomes: Primary outcome: 90-day venous thromboembolism (blood clots in veins). Secondary outcomes: deep vein thrombosis, pulmonary embolism, emergency department visits, readmissions, blood transfusions, pneumonia, and mortality
articles.take_home: Patients with previous blood cancers who took aspirin after hip replacement had fewer blood clots and fewer other complications like needing blood transfusions or developing pneumonia compared with those taking stronger anticoagulant medicines. Aspirin appeared to be a safe and effective option for preventing blood clots in this high-risk group.

articles.title: Adequate canal-filling ratio and extension stem length reduce radiographic loosening in revision total knee arthroplasty using hybrid fixation with grit-blasted, fluted stems.
articles.authors: Shao PW, Lin YC, Chu CT, Chang CH, Hu CC, Lu TW, et al.
articles.population: Patients undergoing revision total knee replacement with minor bone defects using hybrid fixation
articles.outcomes: Radiographic loosening of the knee implant at 24 months or longer follow-up
articles.take_home: Using extension stems that fill at least 83% of the thighbone canal (with length over 20.7 mm) and 77% of the shinbone canal (with length over 18.7 mm) reduces the risk of implant loosening after revision knee replacement surgery. Implants loosened in about 27% of cases overall, but better canal filling and longer stems were protective.

articles.title: Intraoperative hypotension and short-term outcomes after primary total hip arthroplasty : a retrospective cohort study.
articles.authors: Burbelo A, Clark T, Huffman AB, Scarl N, O'Palko S, Ferguson B, et al.
articles.population: Patients undergoing primary total hip arthroplasty
articles.outcomes: 30-day and 90-day readmissions, postoperative length of stay, reoperation within 90 days
articles.take_home: Low blood pressure during hip replacement surgery was not linked to readmission or longer hospital stays, but a relative drop in blood pressure of 20% or more was associated with higher rates of needing another operation within 90 days. Blood loss during surgery was more important than blood pressure in predicting how long patients stayed in hospital.

articles.title: Outcomes of Total Hip Arthroplasty Based on Surgeon Fellowship and Case Volume.
articles.authors: Katanbaf R, Misch M, Aflatooni JO, Smitterberg CW, Griffin D, Nace J, et al.
articles.study_type: Retrospective cohort study using national all-payer database
articles.population: Patients undergoing primary total hip arthroplasty
articles.outcomes: Hip dislocation, aseptic revision, periprosthetic joint infection, aseptic loosening, periprosthetic fracture, surgical site infection, and venous thromboembolism up to two years after surgery
articles.take_home: Surgeons with specialised hip replacement training and high caseloads had lower rates of hip dislocation and need for revision surgery compared to surgeons without this training. However, outcomes for other complications like fracture and blood clots were similar regardless of surgeon training or experience.

articles.title: Functional Outcomes, Complications, and Survivorship After Simultaneous Bilateral Total Hip Arthroplasty: A Retrospective Cohort Study with a Median Follow-up of 4.6 Years.
articles.authors: Diaz Dilernia F, Vasarhelyi E, Lanting B, Naudie D, McCalden R, MacDonald S, et al.
articles.population: Patients undergoing simultaneous bilateral total hip arthroplasty for end-stage bilateral hip disease
articles.outcomes: Functional scores, pain evaluation, complication rates, readmissions, implant survivorship, transfusion requirements
articles.take_home: Having both hip replacements done at the same time improved patients' function and pain significantly, with 95.7% of implants still working well after a median of 4.6 years and only 6.5% experiencing complications.

articles.title: Is a 32 mm glenosphere optimal in reverse shoulder arthroplasty for patients with small glenoid anatomy? : a range of motion simulation study in petite Asian females.
articles.authors: Kiyomatsu H, Fujibuchi T, Kutsuna T, Kinoshita T, Tsuda T, Hayashi M, et al.
articles.population: Asian female patients with small glenoid anatomy undergoing reverse shoulder arthroplasty
articles.sample_size: 11 patients for postoperative outcomes; all patients underwent nine simulations each
articles.outcomes: Impingement-free range of motion in extension-flexion, abduction-adduction, and external-internal rotation across different glenosphere diameters (32 mm, 36 mm, 39 mm) and inferior overhangs (1.5 mm, 3.0 mm, 4.5 mm)
articles.take_home: In patients with small shoulder bone anatomy, using a smaller 32 mm glenosphere with adequate overhang may provide better movement than larger glenospheres. The study was a computer simulation and needs clinical testing to confirm whether this approach works in real patients.

articles.title: Minimal Movement Restrictions Do Not Increase Hip Dislocations Following Total Hip Arthroplasty: A Before-and-After Study of 10,357 Patients.
articles.authors: Klaassen AD, Willigenburg NW, Musters JWQ, Jager J, Poolman RW
articles.population: Patients undergoing total hip arthroplasty
articles.sample_size: 10,357 patients (7,666 with strict restrictions, 2,691 with minimal restrictions)
articles.outcomes: Hip dislocation within 90 days after surgery
articles.take_home: Patients given minimal movement restrictions after hip replacement had similar dislocation rates to those with strict restrictions, suggesting that restricting movements less may be safe and could help patients return to normal activities sooner.
